incorporated info from the German WP
revision.text:
A '''Tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha or reibyō) is a [[Shinto]] [[alter]] which memorialises one's [[ancestor]]s. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate in [[Japan]], Tamayas are found less often in Japanese houses than its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==History== The first Tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
==References== http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[de:Tamaya]]

revision.comment: more from Deutsch
revision.text:
A '''Tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha or reibyō) is a [[Shinto]] [[alter]] which memorialises one's [[ancestor]]s. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate in [[Japan]], Tamayas are found less often in Japanese houses than its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==History== The first Tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period and was strengthened during the [[Kokugaku]] movement. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: added that also for non-relatives
revision.text:
A '''Tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō) is an alter in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A Tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the Tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==History== The first Tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period and was strengthened during the [[Kokugaku]] movement. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto
[[de:Tamaya]]

revision.comment: added ritual section
revision.text:
A '''Tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō) is an alter in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A Tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the Tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== Rites are performed for the Tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first Tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period and was strengthened during the [[Kokugaku]] movement. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto

revision.comment: tamayas became commonplace in ordinary homes
revision.text:
A '''tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō) is an alter in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period. During the [[Kokugaku]] movement it became more common to erect tamayas in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto
[[de:Tamaya]]

revision.comment: added where placed and another reference
revision.text:
A '''tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō) is an alter in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== The tamaya is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, or mitama-san-no-tana, attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the Kamidana.
Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period. During the [[Kokugaku]] movement it became more common to erect tamayas in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''

It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== The tamaya is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, or mitama-san-no-tana, attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the Kamidana.
Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period. During the [[Kokugaku]] movement it became more common to erect tamayas in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''

revision.comment: added info about first tamaya
revision.text:
A '''tamaya''' (jap. 霊屋, spirit windshield frame section; also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō) is an alter in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== The tamaya is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, or mitama-san-no-tana, attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the Kamidana.
Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first tamaya was built in 1599 in the Toyokuni Shrine (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the Kitano Tenmanguu (北野天満宮) shrine. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa]].
Later the Tamaya was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo]] time period. During the [[Kokugaku]] movement it became more common to erect tamayas in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''

It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A tamaya is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly-respected [[Kamidana]].

Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.

During the [[Kokugaku]] movement it became more common to erect tamayas in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''

revision.comment: italics, links etc.
revision.text:
A {{nihongo|'''tamaya'''|霊屋|also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō}} is an altar in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a scroll bearing their names. A ''tamaya'' is used not only to enshrine blood relatives but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the ''tamaya'' is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[Butsudan]].'' Its value is also below the more highly respected ''[[Kamidana]].''
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''Kamidana.''
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine]] (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''

==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine]] (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].

revision.comment: slight editing
revision.text:
A {{nihongo|'''Tamaya'''|霊屋|also otamaya, mitamaya, soreisha, or reibyō}} (English:cornice of the Ghosts) is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] in the [[ancestor worship|ancestor cult]] of the [[Shinto]] dedicated in the memory of the dead ones. It generally has a mirror symbolising the deceased's [[spirit]]s or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rite dominate the [[religion in Japan]], the Tamaya is found less often in Japanese houses as is its Buddhist counterpart, the [[Butsudan]]. Its value is also below the more highly respected [[Kamidana]].
==Ritual== The Tamaya is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the Kamidana.
Rites are performed for the tamaya every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.
==History== The first tamaya was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine]] (豊国神社) in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes. It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).
==References== * http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm * http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287 Okada Yoshiyuki, 2 June 2005 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054 Motegi Sadazumi 24 February 2007 in the Encyclopedia of Shinto * http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm Lafcadio Hearn ''Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation''
[[de:Tamaya]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

Its value is also below the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42-45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|date=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=[[24 February]], [[2007]]|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|date=2001|accessdate=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=[[2 June]], [[2005]]|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|accessdate=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

revision.comment: template
revision.text:
:''for the commune of Niger see [[Tamaya, Niger]]'' A {{nihongo|'''''tamaya'''''|霊屋||also ''otamaya'', ''mitamaya'', ''soreisha'', or ''reibyō''; literally "cornice of the Ghosts"}} is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the [[spirit]]s of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practise]], ''tamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|accessdate=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

:''for the commune of Niger see [[Tamaya, Niger]]'' A {{nihongo|'''''tamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:霊屋|霊屋]]||also ''otamaya'', ''mitamaya'', ''soreisha'', or ''reibyō''; literally "cornice of the Ghosts"}} is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the [[spirit]]s of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''tamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|accessdate=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|accessdate=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.comment: -mention of ''reibyō'' (霊廟) -- that's a mausoleum or shrine, i.e. a building, not something that would go inside another person's house

{{italic title}} :''for the commune of Niger see [[Tamaya, Niger]]'' :''for Tamaya, New Mexico see [[Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)]]'' A {{nihongo|'''''tamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:霊屋|霊屋]]||literally ''tama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called ''otamaya'', ''mitamaya'', or ''soreisha''}} is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the [[spirit]]s of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''tamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== The first ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Architecture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Shinto cult objects]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A Mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''tamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''tamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''tamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[kinensai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ({{Ill|Oyagami|simple}}) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in [[July]] for Urabon-e, and in [[January]].<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes<ref name=":0" />.
The first modern ''tamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''tamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''tamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A Mimitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[kinensai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ({{Ill|Oyagami|simple}}) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in [[July]] for Urabon-e, and in [[January]].<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes<ref name=":0" />.
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links ==
* [http://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Veneration of the dead]]

During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ({{Ill|Oyagami|simple}}) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in [[July]] for Urabon-e, and in [[January]].<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes<ref name=":0" />.

It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[kinensai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ({{Ill|Oyagami|simple}}) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in [[July]] for Urabon-e, and in [[January]].<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links ==
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Veneration of the dead]]

During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links ==

revision.comment: [[User:Ohconfucius/script|Script]]-assisted [[WP:MOS|style]] fixes
revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links ==
* [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Veneration of the dead]]

The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>

During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Authority control}}

The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
