During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Authority control}}

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Mitamaya]]
[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Veneration of the dead]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Mitamaya]]

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Veneration of the dead]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Mitamaya]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Mitamaya]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Mitamaya]]

Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />

Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>

==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Mitamaya]]

In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}

{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}} [[file:Mitamaya.jpg|thumb|A mitamaya]] {{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|[[wiktionary:御霊屋|御霊屋]]||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style [[ancestor worship]], dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] funeral rites dominate in [[religion in Japan|Japanese religious practice]], ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''[[butsudan]]''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>

During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the [[Heian period]] cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired [[Emperor Go-Toba|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of [[Kokugaku]] led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the [[Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|Toyokuni Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]] for [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the [[Edo period]]. During the ''[[Kokugaku]]'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
