revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]]
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}}
{{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead}}, dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} funeral rites dominate in {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious practice}}, ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan}}''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Gotoba}} (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit Ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni Shrine}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. During the ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}}'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Japanese architectural elements}}

It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} funeral rites dominate in {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious practice|1=Religion in Japan}}, ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|WD=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}}''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Gotoba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}} (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit Ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni Shrine|1=Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. During the ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>

The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|WD=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni Shrine|1=Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. During the ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230314175906/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/dummy.html Kinensai explanation] <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Japanese architectural elements}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Tamaya]][[en:Mitamaya]][[ja:霊屋]][[ko:타마야]][[simple:Mitamaya]]
{{Short description|Altar used in Shinto-style ancestor worship}} {{italic title}}
{{for multi|the commune in Niger|Tamaya, Niger|the place in New Mexico|Tamaya (Bernalillo, New Mexico)}} A {{nihongo|'''''mitamaya'''''|||literally ''mitama'' "soul [of the dead]" + ''ya'' "house"; also called, ''otamaya'', ''tamaya'', or ''soreisha'' 祖霊社, or "Reibyo" 霊廟}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9664 |access-date=2023-04-15 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> is an [[altar#Shinto|altar]] used in [[Shinto]]-style {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|qid=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead|1=Veneration of the dead}}, dedicated in the memory of deceased forebears. It generally has a mirror symbolizing the spirits of the deceased or a tablet bearing their names and is used not only to enshrine blood relatives, but also to honor respected non-family members.<ref name="dictionary">{{cite web|url=http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_t.html|title=Basic Terms of Shinto: T|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|year=1997|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
Since {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} funeral rites dominate in {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|qid=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious practice|1=Religion in Japan}}, ''mitamaya'' are found less often in Japanese houses than their Buddhist counterpart, the ''{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|qid=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}}''. Their value are also below that of the more highly respected ''[[kamidana]]''.<ref name="Hearn">{{cite book|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/jai/jai05.htm|first=Lafcadio|last=Hearn|title=Japan, an Attempt at Interpretation|pages=42–45|publisher=The Macmillan Company|location=New York|year=1904}}</ref>
==Ritual== The ''mitamaya'' is placed in an inner chamber, on a shelf, the ''mitama-san-no-tana,'' attached to the wall about six feet high. It is placed lower than the ''kamidana''.<ref name="dictionary"/>
Rites are performed for the ''mitamaya'' every tenth day up to the fiftieth, and thereafter on the one-hundredth day and one-year anniversary. The one-year ritual is followed by another which marks the spirit's joining of the ancestors at the family shrine.<ref name="Shinousai">{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1054|title=Shinsōsai (Shinto Funeral Rites)|last=Motegi|first=Sadazumi|date=24 February 2007|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref>
==History== In ancient times, people held domestic rites called [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] in the February or April and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] in November. During these rites, people worshiped their ancestors, the god of food, and the hearth deity. They believed the spirits of their ancestors ([[Oyagami]]) came to them through the rice.<ref name=":0" />
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} cults of [[Goryō]] developed and people changed to honoring the dead in July for Urabon-e, and in January.<ref name=":0" />
During the medieval period, people started to enshrine the spirits of dead individuals, like Retired {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Emperor Gotoba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}} (1180–1239) whose memory was kept through [[Spirit tablet|memorial tablets]]. In the early modern period, there was a rise in "Shinto funerals" (shinsōsai) where people were treated like kami. The Yoshida clan gave spirit Ranks like Myōjin-gō, Reisha-gō, and Reijin-gō to the dead, who were sometimes worshipped at shrines.<ref name=":0" />
The spread of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} led to an increased interest in Miyamaya, and more people enshrined their ancestors in their homes.<ref name=":0" />
The first modern ''mitamaya'' was built in 1599 in the {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|qid=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni Shrine|1=Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. Screen paintings and its ruins suggest that it was modeled after the [[Kitano Tenmangū|Kitano Tenman-gū]]. It was later destroyed by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]].<ref>{{cite web|title=reibyou 霊廟|publisher=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|year=2001|access-date=2007-08-09|url= http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/r/reibyou.htm}}</ref>
Later the ''mitamaya'' was generally established for Japanese nobles, military heroes, and other people with high reputation. This practice spread in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. During the ''{{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}}'' movement it became more common to erect ''mitamaya'' in ordinary homes.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=287|title=Mitamaya|last=Okada|first=Yoshiyuki|date=2 June 2005|encyclopedia=The Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University|access-date=2007-08-09}}</ref> It formed a central part of the Shinto funeral rituals (神葬祭, shinsōsai).<ref name="Shinousai"/>
==References== {{reflist|2}}
